title: Sample-efficient benchmarking of shallow all-to-all random quantum circuits
authors: Gregory Bentsen, Bill Fefferman, Soumik Ghosh, Michael J. Gullans, Yinchen Liu
abstract: Random circuit sampling (RCS) remains one of the most competitive frameworks for demonstrating quantum advantage in near-term noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware. Unfortunately, absent error-correction, existing benchmarks to characterize these experiments, like linear cross-entropy, have been classically spoofed due to noise. Because of this, there are interesting regimes, like shallow-depth random quantum circuits, where sampling is plausibly classically intractable, but no existing benchmark can distinguish between a noisy quantum computer and an adversarial classical spoofer. In this paper, we demonstrate that the nonlinear cross-entropy provides a sample-efficient benchmark for shallow-depth all-to-all random quantum circuits whose score cleanly separates noisy quantum computers from state-of-the-art classical spoofers, even in the presence of depolarizing noise. Further, we develop a binary classifier based on the notion of heavy output generation that features logarithmic sample complexity at short depth. Our evidence comes from exact analytic expressions for all-to-all Brownian circuit ensembles derived using replica tricks, and numerical simulations that corroborate these results for discrete Haar-random unitary circuits.
summary: This paper proposes a new nonlinear cross-entropy metric to verify quantum advantage in shallow-depth random circuits. It proves that this metric is resistant to classical spoofing and is highly sample-efficient even in the presence of depolarizing noise. The work provides exact analytical solutions for these benchmarks using many-body physics techniques.
main_problem: Existing benchmarks like linear cross-entropy (LXEB) for random circuit sampling can be classically spoofed by noise, making it difficult to verify quantum advantage in shallow-depth circuits.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that nonlinear cross-entropy (log XEB) provides a sample-efficient benchmark that can distinguish noisy quantum computers from classical spoofers, and they develop a HOG-based classifier with logarithmic sample complexity.
method: The study uses the replica trick and large-n mean-field techniques applied to Brownian circuit ensembles, supplemented by numerical simulations of discrete Haar-random unitary circuits.
model_or_system: The model consists of n-qubit all-to-all Brownian quantum circuits with single-qubit depolarizing noise, which can be mapped to a quantum statistical mechanics problem with an effective many-body Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Nonlinear cross-entropy (log XEB), Heavy Output Generation (HOG) fingerprints, and the variance of the XEB score.
important_parameters: Circuit depth (parameterized by a), system size (n), and noise rate (gamma).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes all-to-all connectivity and utilizes the large-n limit and the Brownian (continuous-time) approximation of discrete circuits.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the required number of samples for XEB estimation vs system size; Figure 2 illustrates the mapping to a statistical mechanics problem; Figure 6 shows numerical estimation of expected XEB and variance for discrete circuits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the benchmarking challenge, defines the Brownian circuit model and its mapping to an effective Hamiltonian, derives analytic expressions for the nonlinear XEB and its variance using the replica trick, validates these results with numerical simulations of discrete circuits, and proposes a new HOG-based classifier.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 5. The heavy output generation classifier reliably distinguishes between a clean Brownian circuit, a noisy circuit, and the Harvard spoofing algorithm using only a single bitstring sample. The distribution S(z) over scores z = dq (a) shifts its weight about the decision boundary z∗as we tune the noise rate nγ/12J = 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, ∞ (black to light blue), where n = 100 and a = 1/5. The clean system (γ = 0, black) exhibits ‘score repulsion’ with heavier weight at large scores z > z∗(shaded gray) relative to the classical spoofing algorithm (γ →∞, light blue) whose distribution is dominated by low scores z < z∗.
